sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I can see that I've clicked on the green source area multiple times (rows 10, cols 28-31), but I've never clicked on the yellow source area! Looking at the history, there are no clicks on the yellow source area (rows 28-29, cols 9-12).
Wait, but I did click on (28, 10) which is the red dot inside the yellow source. Let me check if that's considered part of the yellow source area. The yellow source area is rows 28-29, cols 9-12, so (28, 10) is inside this area. But the red dot is a separate object (id=17), so clicking on it might not be the same as clicking on the yellow source.
Let me try clicking on the yellow source pixels themselves, not the red dot. The yellow source pixels are at:
- (28, 9), (28, 11)
- (29, 9), (29, 10), (29, 11)
Wait, but the yellow source is currently at 5 pixels, which means it's a U-shape. Let me check the ascii to see the exact layout.
Actually, looking at the ascii from earlier:
```
row 27: BBGGGB
row 28: BBYrYB
row 29: BBYYYB
```
The yellow pixels are at (28, 10), (28, 12), (29, 10), (29, 11), (29, 12). Wait, that doesn't match the boundary [[28, 9], [29, 10], [28, 11], [29, 11], [29, 9]].
Let me re-check the ascii. The slice was lines[i][7:13], which gives columns 7-12. So:
- row 27: columns 7=B, 8=B, 9=G, 10=G, 11=G, 12=B
- row 28: columns 7=B, 8=B, 9=Y, 10=r, 11=Y, 12=B
- row 29: columns 7=B, 8=B, 9=Y, 10=Y, 11=Y, 12=B
So the yellow pixels are at (28, 9), (28, 11), (29, 9), (29, 10), (29, 11). That's 5 yellow pixels. The red dot is at (28, 10).
Now, I've clicked on (28, 10) which is the red dot, but I haven't clicked on the yellow pixels themselves. Let me try clicking on the yellow source pixels.
But wait, I also haven't clicked on the green source pixels when the green source is at 5 pixels. Let me first set the green source to 5 pixels, then click on it.
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. I've been clicking on the green source area when the green source is at 14 pixels. But maybe I need to click on the green source when it's at 5 pixels, or vice versa.
Let me try a different approach: what if the goal is to click on the SOURCE OBJECTS when they are at SPECIFIC sizes? For example:
- Click on green source when it's at 5 pixels
- Click on yellow source when it's at 14 pixels
Or maybe the goal is to click on the source objects in a specific order, regardless of their sizes.
Let me try clicking on the yellow source pixels first. The yellow source is currently at 5 pixels, with pixels at (28, 9), (28, 11), (29, 9), (29, 10), (29, 11).
Let me click on (29, 10), which is a yellow pixel in the middle of the bottom row of the U-shape.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# I've never clicked on the yellow source pixels themselves (only the red dot at 28,10)
# Let me try clicking on the yellow source pixels
# Yellow source pixels: (28, 9), (28, 11), (29, 9), (29, 10), (29, 11)

action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 29, 'col': 10},  # yellow source pixel
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 10, 'col': 30}   # green source pixel (center)
])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
